name: JavaScript Fundamentals
description: Core JavaScript concepts every senior dev should know cold — closures, prototypes, event loop, and more.

cards.front: What is a closure in JavaScript?
Explain the JavaScript event loop.
cards.back:
The event loop is the mechanism that allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking operations despite being single-threaded.

1. Call stack executes synchronous code
2. Web APIs handle async operations (setTimeout, fetch)
3. Completed callbacks go to the task queue (macrotasks) or microtask queue
4. Microtasks (Promises) run before macrotasks (setTimeout)
5. The event loop checks if the call stack is empty, then pulls from queues

cards.front: What's the difference between var, let, and const?
cards.back:
• var: function-scoped, hoisted (initialized as undefined), can be redeclared
• let: block-scoped, hoisted but NOT initialized (temporal dead zone), cannot be redeclared
• const: block-scoped, must be initialized at declaration, binding cannot be reassigned (but object properties can be mutated)

Best practice: Use const by default, let when rebinding is needed, avoid var.

cards.front: What is prototypal inheritance?
cards.back:
Every JavaScript object has a hidden [[Prototype]] link to another object. When you access a property that doesn't exist on the object, JS walks up the prototype chain.

• Object.create(proto) creates an object with proto as its prototype
• Classes are syntactic sugar over prototypal inheritance
• Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) returns the prototype
• The chain ends at Object.prototype → null

cards.front: What are Promises and how do they differ from callbacks?
cards.back:
A Promise represents the eventual result of an async operation. Unlike callbacks:

• Promises chain with .then() — avoids 'callback hell'
• Promises have 3 states: pending, fulfilled, rejected
• Errors propagate through the chain to .catch()
• Promise.all() runs multiple promises in parallel
• async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises

Callbacks can lead to inversion of control issues; Promises give the caller control back.

cards.front: What is the 'this' keyword and how is its value determined?
cards.back:
The value of 'this' depends on HOW a function is called:

1. Global context: window (browser) or undefined (strict mode)
2. Object method: the object before the dot
3. new keyword: the newly created object
4. call/apply/bind: explicitly set 'this'
5. Arrow functions: inherit 'this' from enclosing lexical scope (no own 'this')

Common gotcha: passing an object method as a callback loses 'this' context.

cards.front: Explain the difference between == and ===.
cards.back:
• == (loose equality): performs type coercion before comparing. '5' == 5 is true.
• === (strict equality): no type coercion — both value AND type must match. '5' === 5 is false.

Coercion rules are complex and error-prone:
• null == undefined is true
• '' == 0 is true
• [] == false is true

Best practice: Always use === to avoid unexpected behavior.

cards.front: What is the difference between shallow and deep copy?
cards.back:
Shallow copy: copies the top-level properties. Nested objects still share references.
• Object.assign({}, obj)
• { ...obj } (spread)
• Array.from(arr) or [...arr]

Deep copy: recursively copies all nested objects.
• structuredClone(obj) (modern, recommended)
• JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) (loses functions, dates, undefined)

Knowing when you need deep vs shallow copy prevents subtle mutation bugs.

cards.front: What are generators in JavaScript?
What is debouncing vs throttling?
cards.back:
Both limit how often a function executes:

Debounce: Waits until a pause in calls. Fires once after the last call + delay.
• Use for: search input, resize handlers, form validation

Throttle: Fires at most once per time interval, regardless of how often it's called.
• Use for: scroll handlers, rate limiting API calls

Example: Typing in a search box — debounce waits until you stop typing. Throttle would fire every 300ms while typing.

cards.front: What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
Explain WeakMap and WeakSet. When would you use them?
cards.back:
WeakMap/WeakSet hold 'weak' references to objects — they don't prevent garbage collection.

WeakMap: keys must be objects, not enumerable
WeakSet: values must be objects, not enumerable

Use cases:
• Private data associated with objects (the data is GC'd when the object is)
• Caching computed values without causing memory leaks
• Tracking DOM elements without preventing cleanup

Key difference from Map/Set: no size property, no iteration, entries are automatically removed when the key object is GC'd.

cards.front: What is event delegation and why is it useful?
What are the different ways to handle errors in async code?
What is the difference between map, filter, and reduce?
cards.back:
All three are array methods that return new arrays (except reduce):

• map: transforms each element → new array of same length
[1,2,3].map(x => x * 2) → [2,4,6]

• filter: keeps elements that pass a test → new array (possibly shorter)
[1,2,3].filter(x => x > 1) → [2,3]

• reduce: accumulates values into a single result
[1,2,3].reduce((sum, x) => sum + x, 0) → 6

reduce is the most powerful — you can implement map and filter with it.

cards.front: What is optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??)?
cards.back:
Optional chaining (?.) safely accesses nested properties without throwing if a value is null/undefined:
user?.address?.street // undefined if any part is null/undefined

Nullish coalescing (??) provides a default for null/undefined only (not '' or 0):
count ?? 10 // 10 only if count is null/undefined
count || 10 // 10 if count is null, undefined, 0, '', false

?? is more precise than || because it doesn't treat falsy values as 'missing'.

cards.front: What is a pure function?
Explain the module systems: CommonJS vs ES Modules.
cards.back:
CommonJS (CJS): Node.js default
• require() / module.exports
• Synchronous loading
• Dynamic (can require conditionally)

ES Modules (ESM): Modern standard
• import / export
• Static analysis (tree-shaking possible)
• Async loading by default
• Named exports + default export

Key differences:
• ESM imports are hoisted and read-only bindings
• CJS exports are value copies; ESM exports are live bindings
• Most modern projects use ESM; Node supports both

cards.front: What is memoization?
What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous iteration?
